citation.title: Comparative study of gravity effects in directional solidification of Al-3.5 wt.% Si and Al-10 wt.% Cu alloys
citation.reference: Zhang, G., Luo, X., Li, Y., et al. (2024). Comparative study of gravity effects in directional solidification of Al-3.5 wt.% Si and Al-10 wt.% Cu alloys. npj Microgravity, 10, 114. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41526-024-00454-9

review_summary: This record extracts benchmark values from Zhang et al. for Al-3.5 wt.% Si directional solidification under matched 1g and drop-tube microgravity conditions.
key_findings: Average DAS decreased from 45.95 um in 1g to 39.49 um in microgravity.
Average eutectic content decreased from 25.46% in 1g to 22.12% in microgravity.
The paper reports matched thermal histories and an approximate cooling rate of 88 K/s for this alloy.
theory_alignment: The comparison links gravity-dependent thermal/solute flow to dendritic scale and eutectic fraction.

citation.title: Comparative study of gravity effects in directional solidification of Al-3.5 wt.% Si and Al-10 wt.% Cu alloys
citation.reference: Zhang, G., Luo, X., Li, Y., et al. (2024). Comparative study of gravity effects in directional solidification of Al-3.5 wt.% Si and Al-10 wt.% Cu alloys. npj Microgravity, 10, 114. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41526-024-00454-9

review_summary: This record extracts benchmark DAS, eutectic-content, and grain-size values for Al-10 wt.% Cu from matched 1g and microgravity samples.
key_findings: Average DAS decreased from 39.33 um in 1g to 35.82 um in microgravity.
Average eutectic content decreased from 51.27% in 1g to 43.73% in microgravity.
Microgravity samples had smaller average grain radii and higher grain number density in both upper and lower statistical regions.
theory_alignment: The Al-Cu benchmark captures how buoyancy of equiaxed grains and Cu-rich melt redistribution influence grain size, DAS, and eutectic content.

citation.title: Benchmark Al-Cu Solidification Experiments in Microgravity and on Earth
citation.reference: Williams, T. J., & Beckermann, C. (2023). Benchmark Al-Cu solidification experiments in microgravity and on Earth. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 54, 405-423. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-022-06909-6

variables.grain_structure_1g: columnar-dominated region from 0 to approximately 3.3 cm, followed by fully equiaxed region

review_summary: Williams and Beckermann provide a direct ISS-vs-Earth Al-Cu benchmark. For Al-4 wt.% Cu, terrestrial solidification produced a progressive CET while the microgravity sample remained fully equiaxed.
key_findings: The Al-4 wt.% Cu 1g sample had a columnar-dominated region from 0 to about 3.3 cm.
The Al-4 wt.% Cu microgravity sample was fully equiaxed.
The 1g sample showed evidence of grain sedimentation.
theory_alignment: This comparison tests gravity-driven solid transport and melt convection effects on CET formation.

citation.title: Benchmark Al-Cu Solidification Experiments in Microgravity and on Earth
citation.reference: Williams, T. J., & Beckermann, C. (2023). Benchmark Al-Cu solidification experiments in microgravity and on Earth. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 54, 405-423. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-022-06909-6

variables.grain_structure_1g: columnar-dominated region from 0 to approximately 2.8 cm, followed by fully equiaxed region
variables.solid_transport_1g: no clear sedimentation or flotation effect

review_summary: For Al-10 wt.% Cu, Williams and Beckermann report a 1g CET near 2.8 cm and a fully equiaxed microgravity sample.
key_findings: The Al-10 wt.% Cu 1g sample had a CET at approximately 2.8 cm.
The Al-10 wt.% Cu microgravity sample was fully equiaxed.
The Al-10 wt.% Cu 1g sample did not show clear grain sedimentation or flotation.
theory_alignment: This neutrally buoyant Al-Cu case helps isolate convection-driven CET effects from strong solid sedimentation or flotation.

citation.title: Benchmark Al-Cu Solidification Experiments in Microgravity and on Earth
citation.reference: Williams, T. J., & Beckermann, C. (2023). Benchmark Al-Cu solidification experiments in microgravity and on Earth. Metallurgical and Materials Transactions A, 54, 405-423. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11661-022-06909-6

variables.grain_structure_1g: large columnar and elongated equiaxed grains near the bottom from 0 to approximately 1.1 cm, followed by equiaxed region

review_summary: For Al-18 wt.% Cu, Williams and Beckermann report a 1g CET near 1.1 cm and evidence of grain flotation, while the microgravity sample was fully equiaxed.
key_findings: The Al-18 wt.% Cu 1g sample had a CET at approximately 1.1 cm.
The Al-18 wt.% Cu microgravity sample was fully equiaxed.
The 1g sample showed evidence of grain flotation.
theory_alignment: This high-Cu benchmark tests the sign reversal of primary-solid buoyancy and its effect on grain transport.

citation.title: Analysis of gravity effects during binary alloy directional solidification by comparison of microgravity and Earth experiments with in situ observation
citation.reference: Bergeon, N., Reinhart, G., Mota, F. L., Mangelinck-Noel, N., & Nguyen-Thi, H. (2021). Analysis of gravity effects during binary alloy directional solidification by comparison of microgravity and Earth experiments with in situ observation. European Physical Journal E. https://doi.org/10.1140/epje/s10189-021-00102-0

review_summary: Bergeon et al. compare Earth and microgravity observations under matched Al-20 wt.% Cu solidification conditions and report gravity-sensitive mushy-zone behavior.
key_findings: The compared Al-20 wt.% Cu experiments used a reported temperature gradient of 15 K/mm and cooling rate of 0.15 K/s.
The paper reports that mushy-zone permeability can be about two times larger for the 1g experiment than for the microgravity experiment.
theory_alignment: This benchmark maps directly to Flemings macrosegregation theory through mushy-zone permeability, dendrite fragmentation, and buoyancy-driven liquid/solid transport.
